content.text: Quantization reduces the number of bits used to represent each weight in a neural network. A 70B model in BF16 (16 bits per value) requires 140 GB of memory — far beyond most hardware. Quantized to 4 bits, the same model fits in 35–40 GB, enabling it to run on consumer GPUs or Mac hardware. The memory bandwidth reduction also speeds up inference because the GPU spends less time reading weights from memory.

attrs.alt: Quantization overview: what it does, four format families (GGUF K-quants, GPTQ, AWQ, bitsandbytes), quality vs memory comparison for 7B model, and decision guide

content.text: A floating-point weight is mapped to an integer by dividing its range into

buckets and recording which bucket each value falls into. At inference, the stored integer is dequantized back to floating-point before the matrix multiplication. The dequantization is cheap; the savings come from reading 4-bit values from memory instead of 16-bit values.
Naïve per-tensor quantization applies one scale factor to all weights in a matrix. Modern formats use

: each small group of weights (e.g. 128 values) gets its own scale factor. This dramatically reduces quantization error because outlier values in one group don't distort the scale for the entire matrix.
, etc.) are mixed-precision: some sensitive layers (attention, embedding) retain higher precision while feed-forward layers use fewer bits. The
(small) suffixes indicate how aggressively the mixed-precision trade-off is applied. K-quants can run on CPU, Metal, or CUDA without any pre-GPU processing.

GPTQ (Generalized Post-Training Quantization) uses a second-order gradient approach: it finds the quantization parameters that minimise the output error of each layer given a calibration dataset. This makes it more accurate than round-to-nearest quantization. GPTQ requires CUDA to run.

# pip install auto-gptq transformers accelerate
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

# Many models have pre-quantized GPTQ versions on HuggingFace
# e.g. TheBloke/<ModelName>-GPTQ
model_name = "TheBloke/Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    device_map="auto",       # spread across available GPUs
    torch_dtype="auto",
)

text = "The capital of Japan is"
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
outputs = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=20, do_sample=False)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
AWQ observes that not all weights are equally important: a small fraction of channels (

) consistently receive large activations and have a disproportionate impact on the output. AWQ scales these channels to a higher magnitude before quantization so they land in better buckets, then scales the activations back. The result is near-GPTQ quality with faster quantization time and better throughput on GPU.

# pip install autoawq transformers accelerate
from awq import AutoAWQForCausalLM
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

model_path = "casperhansen/llama-3-8b-instruct-awq"

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_path)
model = AutoAWQForCausalLM.from_quantized(
    model_path,
    fuse_layers=True,    # fuse attention layers for speed
    device_map="cuda",
)

text = "Explain AWQ in one sentence:"
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").to("cuda")
out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=80, temperature=0.0)
print(tokenizer.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
bitsandbytes integrates with HuggingFace Transformers and quantizes weights at load time — no separate quantization step or pre-quantized model needed.
uses NF4 (Normal Float 4), a non-linear 4-bit format optimized for normally-distributed weights.
uses LLM.int8() for larger 8-bit quantization. Both require CUDA.

# pip install bitsandbytes transformers accelerate
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
import torch

# 4-bit NF4 quantization config (QLoRA standard)
bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,   # nested quantization: quantize the quant constants too
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",        # normal-float 4: optimised for normally-distributed weights
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,  # computation happens in BF16; only storage is 4-bit
)

model_name = "meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    model_name,
    quantization_config=bnb_config,
    device_map="auto",
)

print(f"Model memory: {model.get_memory_footprint() / 1e9:.2f} GB")
# ~5 GB for 8B instead of ~16 GB in BF16

# Inference works identically
text = "Explain NF4 quantization briefly:"
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
outputs = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=100, do_sample=False)
print(tokenizer.decode(outputs[0], skip_special_tokens=True))
vLLM supports AWQ and GPTQ natively. Point it at the HuggingFace repo or local path and specify the quantization format.

from vllm import LLM, SamplingParams

# AWQ: best throughput at 4-bit on GPU
llm_awq = LLM(
    model="casperhansen/llama-3-8b-instruct-awq",
    quantization="awq",
    max_model_len=4096,
)

# GPTQ
llm_gptq = LLM(
    model="TheBloke/Llama-2-7B-Chat-GPTQ",
    quantization="gptq",
    max_model_len=4096,
)

# BnB (quantize at load time)
llm_bnb = LLM(
    model="meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct",
    quantization="bitsandbytes",
    load_format="bitsandbytes",
    max_model_len=4096,
)

params = SamplingParams(temperature=0.0, max_tokens=64)
outputs = llm_awq.generate(["What is quantization?"], params)
print(outputs[0].outputs[0].text)
Quantizing a Model Yourself with GPTQ

When a pre-quantized model is not available, quantize it yourself using AutoGPTQ with a calibration dataset. The dataset is used to measure activation statistics; it doesn't need to match your deployment domain.

# pip install auto-gptq datasets transformers
from auto_gptq import AutoGPTQForCausalLM, BaseQuantizeConfig
from transformers import AutoTokenizer
from datasets import load_dataset

model_id = "meta-llama/Llama-3.2-3B-Instruct"
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_id)

# Calibration data: 128 samples from wikitext
dataset = load_dataset("wikitext", "wikitext-2-raw-v1", split="train")
calibration = [
    tokenizer(ex["text"], return_tensors="pt").input_ids.squeeze()
    for ex in dataset.select(range(128))
    if len(ex["text"]) > 100
][:128]

quant_config = BaseQuantizeConfig(
    bits=4,
    group_size=128,      # per-group quantization
    desc_act=False,      # disable for faster inference
)

model = AutoGPTQForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, quant_config)
model.quantize(calibration)   # takes ~5–20 min on GPU

# Save the quantized model
model.save_quantized("./llama-3.2-3b-gptq")
tokenizer.save_pretrained("./llama-3.2-3b-gptq")
Perplexity on a held-out text corpus is a fast proxy for quantization quality. Lower perplexity = less degradation from the original model.

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM
from datasets import load_dataset
import math

def compute_perplexity(
    model_path: str,
    quantization_config=None,
    n_samples: int = 50,
    stride: int = 512,
    max_length: int = 1024,
) -> float:
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_path)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_path,
        quantization_config=quantization_config,
        device_map="auto",
        torch_dtype=torch.float16,
    )
    model.eval()

    dataset = load_dataset("wikitext", "wikitext-2-raw-v1", split="test")
    encodings = tokenizer(" ".join(dataset["text"][:n_samples]), return_tensors="pt")
    input_ids = encodings.input_ids.to(model.device)

    nlls = []
    for begin in range(0, input_ids.size(1) - max_length, stride):
        end = begin + max_length
        target_ids = input_ids[:, begin:end].clone()
        with torch.no_grad():
            loss = model(input_ids=input_ids[:, begin:end], labels=target_ids).loss
        nlls.append(loss.item())

    return math.exp(sum(nlls) / len(nlls))

# Example comparison:
# BF16 Llama-3.2-3B → perplexity ~7.8
# Q4_K_M GGUF       → perplexity ~8.1  (3.8% worse)
# Q2_K GGUF         → perplexity ~10.4 (33% worse)
CPU-capable, best ratio of quality to size
Best throughput per VRAM on CUDA hardware
bitsandbytes supports backprop through quantized weights
Quick prototype, no model file prepared
Works with any HuggingFace model, no conversion step
Most popular models have pre-quantized versions on HuggingFace. Common publishers:
— GGUF K-quant versions of most popular models.
— large archive of GGUF, GPTQ, and AWQ models (older, but extensive).
— Meta, Mistral, Google now publish official GGUF versions alongside the original safetensors files.

from huggingface_hub import hf_hub_download, list_repo_files

def list_gguf_files(repo_id: str) -> list[str]:
    """List available GGUF quantizations for a repo."""
    return [
        f for f in list_repo_files(repo_id)
        if f.endswith(".gguf")
    ]

# Example: find all GGUF files for a bartowski model
files = list_gguf_files("bartowski/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-GGUF")
for f in sorted(files):
    print(f)
# Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q2_K.gguf
# Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf
# Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q5_K_M.gguf
# Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q8_0.gguf
# ...

# Download the Q4_K_M version
path = hf_hub_download(
    repo_id="bartowski/Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-GGUF",
    filename="Meta-Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct-Q4_K_M.gguf",
    local_dir="./models",
)
: These formats require CUDA kernels for dequantization. They cannot run on CPU or Metal. Use GGUF for non-CUDA hardware.
: Perplexity on wikitext does not predict quality on code generation or instruction following. Run task-specific benchmarks for your use case.
: Simple round-to-nearest (RTN) quantization skips the calibration step. It is fast but produces noticeably lower quality than GPTQ or AWQ for the same bit width.
: The GGUF file size is not the same as the VRAM needed. The runtime also allocates KV cache and context buffers. For a 4.1 GB Q4_K_M file with

Before quantization became practical, running open-weight language models locally required expensive server-grade hardware. A 70B parameter model in full BF16 precision demands 140 GB of GPU memory — that means at least four A100 80GB GPUs, costing tens of thousands of dollars. Quantization broke this barrier: the same model at 4-bit precision fits in 35–40 GB, which is achievable with two consumer GPUs or on high-VRAM workstations.

For AI practitioners, quantized local models solve several concrete pain points. API-dependent workflows fail when providers have outages, introduce latency from network round-trips (50–200ms per request), and expose proprietary data to third-party servers — a compliance problem in healthcare, finance, and legal applications. Running a quantized Llama-3 locally gives sub-10ms time-to-first-token latency, full data privacy, and zero per-token cost. For iterative development and batch inference over large datasets, the economics strongly favour local deployment.
Memory reduction proportional to bit width.
Quantizing from 16-bit to 4-bit cuts weight storage by 4x. A 7B model drops from ~14 GB to ~4 GB — fitting on a single consumer GPU with memory left over for the KV cache.

LLM inference is memory-bandwidth-bound, not compute-bound, for batch sizes below ~8. Reading 4-bit weights from VRAM instead of 16-bit directly halves the bandwidth requirement, yielding ~2x tokens/second throughput.
Modern K-quants and AWQ achieve 96–98% of the original model's benchmark scores at 4-bit precision. For most practical tasks, the quality difference is imperceptible.
GGUF Q4_K_M models run on CPU-only hardware via llama.cpp. A 7B model generates ~15 tok/s on a modern laptop CPU — slow by GPU standards, but sufficient for batch processing and development.
No API cost for batch workloads.
Processing 1 million tokens via a cloud API costs $1–15. Running locally costs only electricity — roughly $0.001–0.01 per million tokens depending on hardware.
Sensitive documents (medical records, legal contracts, source code) never leave the machine. Critical for HIPAA, GDPR, and IP-protection requirements.
Quality degrades at aggressive bit widths.

At 2-bit (Q2_K), perplexity increases 30–40% over the original. The model becomes confused on complex reasoning, code generation, and low-frequency vocabulary. 4-bit is the practical minimum for quality work.

Even with a quantized model, the KV cache grows linearly with context length. A 7B model at Q4_K_M with 32K context uses ~4 GB for the model + ~8 GB for the KV cache — exceeding 8 GB VRAM at long contexts.
GGUF models run only on llama.cpp-based tools. GPTQ and AWQ require CUDA. You cannot mix runtimes — choosing a format locks you to specific inference stacks.
Quantization can't fix a weak base model.
A 7B model at Q8_0 still has lower capability than a 13B model at Q4_K_M. Quantization preserves a percentage of the base quality — it cannot add capability that wasn't there.
Fine-tuning quantized models requires special setup.
Standard fine-tuning on a quantized model is not possible. QLoRA (with bitsandbytes NF4) is the standard workaround, but it adds complexity.
When to use quantized local models
Processing sensitive data that cannot leave your infrastructure (medical, legal, financial).
High-volume batch inference where API costs would be prohibitive.
Development and experimentation where API round-trip latency disrupts iteration speed.
Air-gapped or offline environments (edge devices, secure facilities, remote deployments).
Running an open-weight model that has no hosted API equivalent.
Tasks requiring state-of-the-art capability (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) where no comparable open model exists.
Low request volume where hardware acquisition cost exceeds API bills.
Production serving with dynamic scaling needs (cloud auto-scales; local hardware is fixed).
Start with Q4_K_M for new projects.
It gives the best quality-per-GB ratio among GGUF formats and is the community default. Only go lower (Q3_K_M) if you're VRAM-constrained.
Run a 50-sample wikitext perplexity check on your chosen quant and compare to the F16 baseline. If PPL degrades > 5%, move to a higher bit width.
Use Ollama for the simplest local deployment.
Ollama handles model download, format conversion, and serving with a single ollama run model-name command — no Python required.
The model file size understates total VRAM use. Add 15–25% for KV cache at typical context lengths (2K–8K). A 4.1 GB Q4_K_M model needs ~5.5 GB VRAM total at 4K context.
For production GPU serving, prefer vLLM with AWQ.
vLLM's PagedAttention + AWQ gives the best tokens/second/GPU across batch sizes. GPTQ is a close second.
Throughput benchmarks for a 7B model on representative hardware (approximate, varies by exact model and context length):

Generation speed (tokens/second) is memory-bandwidth-bound at batch size 1. Throughput scales roughly with GPU memory bandwidth: an RTX 4090 (1008 GB/s) generates ~1.7x faster than an RTX 3090 (936 GB/s) for the same quantization level. For batch serving, vLLM's PagedAttention allows batching requests efficiently, increasing throughput proportionally with batch size up to VRAM saturation.
Simplest local LLM serving, REST API included
Quantize models yourself using GPTQ algorithm
Quantize models yourself using AWQ algorithm
Desktop GUI for downloading and running GGUF models

Compromised model weights on HuggingFace Hub or third-party mirrors are a supply-chain attack vector. Always verify SHA256 checksums against the canonical repository before loading a model in production.
Model weights do not have runtime code execution permissions.
Unlike Python pickle files, safetensors files (used by HuggingFace) cannot execute arbitrary code on load. Prefer safetensors over .bin (pickle) format for security.
Quantization does not remove training data memorisation.
A quantized model retains much of the original model's training data memorisation. If the base model can reproduce sensitive training data, the quantized version likely can too.
Serve behind authentication if exposing a local API.

Ollama and vLLM serve on localhost by default, which is safe. If binding to 0.0.0.0 for network access, add authentication (API key or mTLS) — unauthenticated LLM endpoints are a common security gap in enterprise AI deployments.
Quantization reduces weight precision from 16/32-bit to 4–8 bits, cutting memory 2–4x with 2–5% quality loss at 4-bit precision — the standard deployment target.
Q4_K_M (GGUF) is the recommended default for CPU/Mac/offline use; AWQ 4-bit is recommended for CUDA GPU serving with highest throughput.
GPTQ minimises per-layer quantization error using a calibration dataset; AWQ protects high-activation (salient) channels; bitsandbytes quantises at load time from any HuggingFace model.
Generation speed is memory-bandwidth-bound at batch size 1: 4-bit weights read from VRAM twice as fast as 16-bit, giving ~2x throughput on the same GPU.
Measure perplexity (lower = better) before deployment to validate that your chosen quant level does not unacceptably degrade quality.
Verify model checksums, prefer safetensors over pickle files, and add authentication before exposing a local inference API to the network.
